docs.title: Revfore Accelerate
docs.text:
A no-code relational framework for OneStream that helps developers and administrators build, manage, and surface relational data solutions faster.

Revfore Accelerate simplifies how relational tables, relationships, views, and governed user interfaces are created within OneStream. Instead of building everything from scratch with custom SQL, dashboards, and workflows, teams can use Revfore Accelerate to deliver relational data solutions more quickly and consistently.

docs.title: What is Revfore Accelerate?
docs.text:
Revfore Accelerate is designed for OneStream solution development that depends on relational data. It provides a configurable framework for defining and managing:

  • Relational tables
  • Relational models
  • Relational views
  • Editable lists and grids
  • Dashboard-driven user experiences
  • Administrative settings and metadata

This makes it easier to support operational planning, workflow-driven applications, reference data management, and other custom relational use cases inside OneStream.

docs.title: Why use Revfore Accelerate?
docs.text:
Revfore Accelerate helps teams:

  • Reduce custom development effort
  • Standardize relational solution design
  • Build new relational data structures faster
  • Surface data in governed, reusable interfaces
  • Support both administrators and end users with consistent experiences
  • Extend OneStream for planning, workflows, and custom business processes

docs.title: Common use cases
docs.text:
Revfore Accelerate can support a wide range of solutions, including:

  • Operational planning applications
  • Detailed financial planning
  • Reference and master data management
  • Workflow-based data collection
  • Custom administrative applications
  • Browser-based and dashboard-driven relational interfaces

docs.title: Relational Tables
docs.text: Create and manage relational tables, fields, and relationships through a configurable framework.

docs.title: Relational Models
docs.text: Unify data across tables with lookups, defaults, required fields, and calculated columns

docs.title: Relational Views
docs.text: Define relational views that can be used across dashboards, editable lists, and other user experiences.

docs.title: Governed user interfaces
docs.text: Surface relational data in standardized, governed screens for both setup and end-user processes.

docs.title: Faster solution development
docs.text: Reduce the time and complexity required to build custom relational solutions in OneStream.

docs.title: Who is this documentation for?
docs.text:
This documentation is intended for:

  • OneStream developers
  • Solution architects
  • Application administrators
  • Technical consultants
  • Power users supporting relational solutions

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text:
Use the navigation menu to explore the documentation. A typical path might include:

  1. Overview of the platform
  2. Core concepts and architecture
  3. Configuration and setup
  4. Managing tables, relationships, and views
  5. Building end-user experiences
  6. Deployment and administration

docs.title: Documentation sections
docs.text:
Suggested sections for this site:

  • Overview — Product introduction and key concepts
  • Architecture — How the framework is structured
  • Configuration — Setup instructions and prerequisites
  • Tables and Fields — Defining relational structures
  • Relationships — Linking relational data
  • Views — Building reusable relational views
  • User Interfaces — Editable lists, dashboards, and navigation
  • Administration — Managing metadata and settings
  • Examples — Sample use cases and implementation patterns
  • Troubleshooting — Common issues and resolutions

docs.title: Revfore Accelerate at a glance
docs.text: Revfore Accelerate helps organizations extend OneStream with a faster, more governed approach to relational solution development — making it easier to deliver applications that are flexible, maintainable, and scalable.

docs.title: Getting Started with Revfore Accelerate
docs.text:
Welcome to Revfore Accelerate.

This guide will help you understand the core concepts and get up and running with building relational data solutions inside OneStream.

docs.title: 🚀 What is Revfore Accelerate?
docs.text:
Revfore Accelerate is a no-code relational framework that allows you to:

  • Create relational tables along with their relationships to other tables
  • Create relational models that source data from any number of relational tables
  • Build reusable relational views from the relational models along with allowed user actions
  • Surface data in editable grids and dashboards

All without writing SQL.

docs.title: ⚙️ Setup
docs.text:
Before getting started, make sure your environment is ready:

👉 Follow the Setup And Installation to install and configure Revfore Accelerate.

docs.title: 🧠 Core Concepts
docs.text: Before you begin, it’s helpful to understand a few key ideas:

docs.title: Relational Tables
docs.text: Structured datasets that store your business data and define how that data links together through relationships (e.g., Customers, Rates, Employees, Assets, Products, Requests)

docs.title: Relational Models
docs.text: Unified structures that bring data from multiple tables into one place. Models allow you to define lookups, defaults, required fields, and additional expression-based columns to simplify data entry and enforce consistency

docs.title: Relational Views
docs.text: Reusable, queryable representations of your relational data. Views are also where you define what actions users can take, such as adding, editing, or managing data

docs.title: Metadata-Driven UI
docs.text: Core structures are defined in metadata, enabling consistent, automatically generated interfaces that can be leveraged in OneStream Genesis, Partner Solutions, and custom solutions to deliver user-friendly applications and dashboards

docs.title: ⚙️ Basic Workflow
docs.text:
Here’s the typical process for building a solution:

  1. Create tables and relationships

  2. Define your data structures and how they link together

  3. Build relational models

  4. Unify data across tables with lookups, defaults, required fields, and calculated columns

  5. Build views

  6. Create reusable, queryable data outputs, define filters, and control available user actions

  7. Expose in UI

  8. Use dashboards and grids for interaction

docs.title: 🏁 Your First Use Case
docs.text:
A common starting point:

Build a rate table for products or services

Example: - Create a Product or Service table - Create a Rate table to store pricing or cost details - Define rate-related attributes (e.g., unit price, cost, effective dates) - Link to other tables (e.g., customers, regions, or units of measure) - Build relational models unify data and to apply lookups, defaults, and required fields - Create views with filters and user actions for managing and maintaining the data

docs.title: 💡 Tips
docs.text:
• Start simple — one table at a time
  • Think in terms of business entities (not technical structures)
  • Reuse views whenever possible
  • Keep naming consistent

docs.title: 👉 Next Steps
docs.text:
• Learn about Relational Tables
  • Understand Relational Models
  • Explore Relational Views

docs.title: Admin
docs.text:
The Admin application group is used for day-to-day administration of relational data structures in Revfore Accelerate.

This is where users define, manage, and maintain the core components that power relational planning and operational workflows.

While the Config application group is focused on setup, upgrades, and system-level tasks, the Admin application group is where users actively build and manage their data structures.

docs.title: Admin Sections
docs.text:
The Admin application group includes the following sections:

  • Relational Tables
  • Relational Models
  • Relational Views
  • Lookups
  • Forms

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Use the Admin application group to:

  • define and manage relational data structures
  • organize relationships between entities
  • create reusable data views
  • manage supporting lookup data
  • configure forms for user interaction and data entry

docs.title: How the Sections Work Together
docs.text: The Admin components are designed to work together as part of a relational framework:

docs.title: Relational Tables
docs.text: Define the core business entities and store detailed data.

docs.title: Relational Models
docs.text: Connect tables together and define relationships between entities.

docs.title: Relational Views
docs.text: Provide reusable, queryable outputs of relational data for reporting and application use.

docs.title: Lookups
docs.text: Support standardized values and controlled lists used across tables and forms.

docs.title: Forms
docs.text: Provide user-facing interfaces for entering and managing data.

docs.title: Typical Workflow
docs.text:
A common workflow in the Admin application group:

  1. Create Relational Tables to define your data structure
  2. Build Relational Models to link those tables together
  3. Define Relational Views to expose and use the data
  4. Configure Lookups for controlled values
  5. Use Forms to allow users to interact with the data

docs.title: When to Use Admin
docs.text:
Use the Admin application group when:

  • building or modifying relational data structures
  • configuring planning or operational models
  • managing data definitions and relationships
  • creating user-facing data entry or interaction experiences

docs.title: Related Application Groups
docs.text:
• Use Config for setup, upgrades, licensing, and system-level tasks
  • Use Home to expose relational views in navigation pages
  • Use Help for documentation and support resources

docs.title: Forms
docs.text:
← Back to Admin

Forms provide user-facing interfaces for entering, editing, and managing data.

They allow users to interact with relational data in a structured and intuitive way.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Use Forms to:

  • capture user input
  • edit relational data
  • create guided data entry experiences
  • interact with Relational Tables and Views

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Forms are built on top of Relational Tables and Views
  • Forms provide structured input interfaces
  • Forms can include validation and controlled inputs

docs.title: Typical Use Cases
docs.text:
• Data entry for planning models
  • Managing operational data
  • Updating relational records
  • Guided workflows for users

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Forms should be designed for ease of use
  • Use Lookups to control input values
  • Keep forms focused on a specific task or entity

docs.title: Lookups
docs.text:
← Back to Admin

Lookups are used to define standardized values and controlled lists used throughout the solution.

They help ensure consistency and improve data quality.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Use Lookups to:

  • define controlled value lists
  • standardize input values across tables and forms
  • improve data consistency and governance

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Lookups provide predefined values
  • Lookups are reusable across tables and forms
  • Lookups help prevent inconsistent or invalid data entry

docs.title: Typical Use Cases
docs.text:
• Status values (Active, Inactive)
  • Categories or classifications
  • Dropdown selections in forms
  • Standardized codes and labels

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Use Lookups wherever consistent values are required
  • Keep lookup lists simple and well-defined
  • Reuse existing lookups when possible

docs.title: Relational Models
docs.text:
← Back to Admin

Relational Models define how Relational Tables are connected and interact with each other.

They establish relationships between entities and form the structure of your overall data model.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Use Relational Models to:

  • define relationships between tables
  • organize data into a cohesive structure
  • enable meaningful data interaction across entities

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Models connect tables using defined relationships
  • Relationships determine how data flows between tables
  • Models provide the structure used by Relational Views

docs.title: Typical Use Cases
docs.text:
• Linking Employees to Departments
  • Connecting Assets to Locations
  • Relating Products to Service Lines

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Models should reflect real-world relationships
  • Keep relationships simple and well-defined
  • A well-designed model improves flexibility and performance

docs.title: Relational Tables
docs.text:
← Back to Admin

Relational Tables are the foundation of the Revfore Accelerate data model.

They define the core business entities and store data at its natural level of detail.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Use Relational Tables to:

  • define business entities (e.g., Employees, Assets, Products)
  • store detailed operational and planning data
  • structure data in a flexible, relational format

Each table consists of:

  • Columns (fields) – attributes of the entity
  • Rows (records) – individual instances of the entity

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Tables are defined using metadata (no SQL required)
  • Tables are reusable across models, views, and forms
  • Tables represent real-world business entities

docs.title: Typical Use Cases
docs.text:
• Employee planning
  • Asset tracking
  • Product and service modeling
  • Operational data capture

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Tables should be designed around clear business concepts
  • Avoid combining unrelated data into a single table
  • Keep naming consistent and meaningful

docs.title: Relational Views
docs.text:
← Back to Admin

Relational Views provide reusable, queryable representations of your relational data.

They allow you to combine and present data from multiple tables in a structured format.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Use Relational Views to:

  • create reusable data outputs
  • combine data from multiple tables
  • expose data for reporting and application use
  • integrate data into dashboards and pages

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Views are built on top of Relational Models
  • Views define how data is displayed and accessed
  • Views can be used in forms, dashboards, and navigation pages

docs.title: Typical Use Cases
docs.text:
• Reporting views
  • Planning data outputs
  • Data integration into Genesis pages
  • User-facing data displays

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Views should be designed for reuse
  • Keep logic clear and maintainable
  • Views are often the primary way users interact with relational data

docs.title: Config
docs.text:
The Config application group is used for non-daily administrative and system-level tasks in Revfore Accelerate.

Where the Admin application group is focused on routine daily administration, the Config application group is intended for setup, upgrades, licensing, maintenance, and other less frequent actions that help install, maintain, or troubleshoot the solution.

docs.title: Config Sections
docs.text:
The Config application group includes the following sections:

  • Manage License Key
  • Setup & Upgrade
  • Uninstall
  • Run Maintenance Scripts
  • Other

docs.title: When to Use Config
docs.text:
Use the Config application group when you need to:

  • set up Revfore Accelerate for the first time
  • upgrade to a newer version
  • manage Starter or Full license keys
  • uninstall some or all of the solution
  • run maintenance or support-provided scripts
  • perform occasional system-wide synchronization or relinking tasks

docs.title: Related Application Groups
docs.text: For routine ongoing administration, go to the Admin application group, where users can review and manage existing Relational Tables, Models, and Views.

docs.title: Manage License Key
docs.text:
← Back to Config Summary

The Manage License Key section is used to retrieve the customer reference code, enter license keys, and validate the current license status for Revfore Accelerate.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Revfore Accelerate supports two types of license keys:

  • Starter
  • Full

Both license types allow access to the designer experience, but they differ in how they are requested and what areas of the solution they unlock.

docs.title: Customer Reference Code
docs.text:
The customer reference code is a unique number for a specific OneStream environment.

It consists of two parts: 1) The application customer name found in Application | Application Properties | Customer Name. 2) A unique environment value that is auto generated after the solution is installed.

NOTE: A 'Starter' key can be generated from just the Customer Name.

This code is used to generate a Full license key and is only available after Revfore Accelerate has been installed.

docs.title: Starter License Keys
docs.text:
A Starter license key can be requested before Revfore Accelerate is installed.

It only requires the customer name, which can be found on the Application Properties page in OneStream.

docs.title: Starter Key Benefits
docs.text:
Starter keys allow users to begin using Revfore Accelerate immediately rather than waiting for a Full license key.

With a Starter key, users can access the designer pages and work with:

  • Relational Table definitions
  • Relational Model definitions
  • Relational View definitions

docs.title: Full License Keys
docs.text: A Full license key requires the customer reference code, which is only available after the solution has been installed.

docs.title: Full Key Benefits
docs.text: A Full license key allows users to do everything a Starter key allows, plus gain access to the end user navigation pages.

docs.title: Common Tasks
docs.text:
Use this section to:

  • retrieve the customer reference code
  • enter a new license key
  • validate a Starter or Full license key
  • confirm what level of access the current key provides

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Starter keys are helpful when you want to begin setup and design work quickly.
  • Full keys are required for full solution access, including end user navigation pages.

docs.title: Other
docs.text:
← Back to Config Summary

The Other section includes additional system-level actions that administrators can run as needed.

These actions are generally safe to run and are helpful for synchronizing metadata or relinking records after installs or upgrades.

docs.title: Sync All Managed Relational Views
docs.text: This action loops through all managed relational views and syncs their definitions with the actual SQL views in the database.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
This is helpful when:

  • you have made changes to relational view definitions
  • you want to ensure those changes have been applied to the database
  • you want to confirm that all managed SQL views are in sync

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This action is safe to run anytime.
  • The system automatically runs this during setup and after any upgrade.

docs.title: Relink Forms and Users
docs.text:
Forms and Users are linked using both:

  • their hidden unique IDs
  • their names

This process takes the names and updates the unique IDs.

Revfore Accelerate normally uses the unique IDs, so these records need to be relinked after an install or upgrade.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this action when:

  • forms or users need to be relinked after an install
  • forms or users need to be relinked after an upgrade
  • the automatic relinking process did not complete as expected

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This process should automatically run, but it can also be run manually here if needed.
  • It is safe to run anytime.
  • Revfore Accelerate automatically creates user records when it encounters a new user login.

docs.title: Run Maintenance Scripts
docs.text:
← Back to Config Summary

The Run Maintenance Scripts section is used to execute database scripts provided by the Revfore support team.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Use this section when Revfore provides a script to fix a specific non-routine database issue.

These scripts are typically intended to address targeted maintenance or support cases and are not part of normal day-to-day administration.

docs.title: When to Use This Section
docs.text:
Use this section only when:

  • Revfore support has provided a script
  • you need to address a non-routine database issue
  • you have been instructed to run a specific maintenance action

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This section is not intended for routine administration.
  • Only run scripts from the Revfore support team.
  • Be sure to understand the purpose of a script before running it in an environment.

docs.title: Setup & Upgrade
docs.text:
← Back to Config Summary

The Setup & Upgrade section is used to install Revfore Accelerate for the first time and to upgrade it later to a newer version.

The available options change depending on whether the database has already been set up or not.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Use this section to:

  • perform the initial setup of the solution
  • create the required database objects
  • launch the solution after setup
  • prepare and process upgrade files
  • upgrade the database for a newer version

docs.title: Step 1: Setup Database
docs.text:
Use Setup Database to create the initial database structures required by Revfore Accelerate.

This includes items such as:

  • schemas
  • tables
  • stored procedures
  • views
  • default data

This step prepares the environment for the solution to run.

docs.title: Step 2: Launch Solution
docs.text:
After the database setup is complete, the solution is ready to use.

At that point, users will typically go to the Admin application group to begin reviewing existing and creating new:

  • Relational Tables
  • Relational Models
  • Relational Views

docs.title: Upgrade
docs.text: When upgrading Revfore Accelerate, both the application components and the database may need to be updated.

docs.title: Step 1: Create Upgrade File, Uninstall, and Load
docs.text:
To upgrade Revfore Accelerate, you must must create an upgrade file from a full install package.

Step 1a: Download and Unzip the Revfore Accelerate full install zip file

  • Download the desired version of Revfore Accelerate from the OneStream Solution Exchange
  • Unzip it and extract the ApplicationWorkspaces.xml file

Step 1b: Choose which RFA maintenance units you want to upgrade

You have the option of - both Core and Custom RFA maintenance units, or - only the Core RFA maintenance units

Leave the checkbox checked if you want both upgrade. Uncheck it if you only want to upgrade the Core RFA maintenance units

Note

The Genesis maintenance units are always ignored during a Revfore Accelerate upgrade.

If you want to upgrade the Genesis maintenance units, go to the Revfore Accelerate Designer page and then navigate to:

  1. Settings
  2. Instance Management
  3. Upgrade

Step 1c: Create the upgrade file

  • Click on Select & Create Xml File
  • Select the ApplicationWorkspaces.xml file you extracted
  • This will create an upgrade xml file and save it to your OneStream file explorer. The path should be provided.

Step 1d: Uninstall RFA Maintenance Units

  • Click on Uninstall RFA Maintenance Units
  • This will uninstall the RFA Maintenance Units you choose to remove

Step 1e: Download the upgrade xml file and load it

  • Take note of the provided path
  • Go to the OneStream File Explorer, by clicking on the File Explorer at the top left of you OneStream application
  • Locate the file and download it to a local folder
  • Load the upgrade xml file through the Application tab | Tools | Load/Extract process in OneStream

docs.title: Step 2: Upgrade Database
docs.text:
Click on Upgrade Database to apply any database changes required for the upgraded version.

  • This step updates the database structures as needed to align with the new application version.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The Setup & Upgrade section is the primary place to install and upgrade Revfore Accelerate.
  • In most upgrade scenarios, this section should be used instead of manually uninstalling maintenance units.
  • Available actions may vary depending on the current database state and version.

docs.title: Uninstall
docs.text:
← Back to Config Summary

The Uninstall section is used to remove some or all of Revfore Accelerate.

There are two uninstall options, depending on whether you want to remove only the application components or completely remove the entire solution.

docs.title: Option 1: Uninstall UI / Business Rules
docs.text:
This option removes the UI and Business Rules stored in maintenance units while leaving the database in place.

This is typically used as part of an upgrade-related process where application components need to be removed and reloaded.

You can choose a subset of maintenance units to remove.

docs.title: Typical Use
docs.text:
In most cases, users should use the Upgrade option in Setup & Upgrade instead of this uninstall option.

Use this option only if directed by Revfore.

docs.title: Option 2: Uninstall Full
docs.text:
This option removes everything, including:

  • the database
  • all maintenance units
  • any custom work that has been done in the solution

docs.title: Use This Option Only When
docs.text:
Use Uninstall Full only when:

  • you are no longer using the solution, or
  • you want to fully reset the environment by uninstalling and reinstalling it

docs.title: Warning
docs.text: A full uninstall removes all solution components, including customizations. Make sure you fully understand the impact before proceeding.

docs.title: Help
docs.text:
The Help application group provides quick access to documentation and support resources for Revfore Accelerate.

Use this application group when you need to review product documentation or contact the Revfore support team.

docs.title: Available Pages
docs.text:
The Help application group includes the following pages:

  • Documentation
  • Contact Support

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
The Documentation page provides an embedded web browser experience that links directly to the Revfore Accelerate documentation hosted online.

This allows users to review the latest available documentation without leaving the solution.

docs.title: Typical Uses
docs.text:
Use the Documentation page to:

  • review setup and configuration instructions
  • understand core concepts and features
  • reference designer and admin guidance
  • access the latest published product documentation

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The page displays the online documentation inside an embedded browser experience.
  • Documentation content is maintained separately from the application and can be updated as new versions are published.

docs.title: Contact Support
docs.text:
The Contact Support page provides access to a web page where users can contact the Revfore support team.

Use this page when you need help with issues, questions, troubleshooting, or guidance related to Revfore Accelerate.

docs.title: Typical Uses
docs.text:
Use the Contact Support page to:

  • request help with an issue
  • ask product or setup questions
  • get assistance with non-routine maintenance needs
  • contact Revfore for troubleshooting support

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This page opens a support web page for contacting Revfore.
  • For database maintenance scripts or other guided fixes, follow instructions provided by the Revfore support team.

docs.title: Related Information
docs.text:
For product documentation, use the Documentation page.

For direct assistance, use the Contact Support page.

docs.title: Home Application Group
docs.text:
The Home Application Group is used to create and manage custom navigation groups and pages within a Genesis workspace.

It provides a flexible way to build user-facing navigation and integrate Relational Views created in Revfore Accelerate.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Use the Home Application Group to:

  • create custom navigation groups
  • add custom pages
  • integrate Relational Views into user-facing pages
  • extend existing Genesis workspaces with additional functionality

You can also add custom navigation groups and pages to any Genesis workspace, not just the Home Application Group.

docs.title: When to Use the Home Application Group
docs.text:
The Home Application Group is best suited for:

  • adding a limited number of navigation groups and pages
  • extending an existing solution with a small number of custom pages
  • quickly exposing Relational Views to end users

docs.title: When to Use a Separate Instance
docs.text:
If you need to:

  • create a large number of navigation groups and pages
  • build a full solution experience
  • leverage additional application groups such as:
  • Config
  • Admin
  • Help

👉 then you should create a separate Genesis instance

This approach is recommended when building a complete standalone solution.

docs.title: Integrating Relational Views into Genesis Pages
docs.text:
You can add a Revfore Accelerate Relational View to any page in any Genesis instance.

This allows you to surface relational data directly within your application’s navigation.

docs.title: Steps: Add a Relational View to a Genesis Page
docs.text:
Add a Revfore Accelerate Relational View

  1. Go to your desired Genesis Instance Designer.

  2. Add a page to any Navigation Group.

  3. Select "Link an Existing Content Item from a Shared Workspace".

  4. Select the "Revfore Accelerate (RFA)" workspace.

  5. In the filter field, type:

ContentItem

  1. Select one of the following dashboards:

  2. XCP_RfaConItm100_Set1000 – Single Content Sub Item

  3. XCP_RfaConItm101_Set1000 – Two Vertical Content Sub Items

  4. XCP_RfaConItm102_Set1000 – Two Horizontal Content Sub Items

  5. Select the Content tab.

  6. Click Configure to set up the desired Relational View.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• These dashboards are prebuilt to host Relational Views within Genesis pages.
  • The layout you choose determines how the content is displayed (single, vertical split, or horizontal split).
  • Relational Views must already be defined in Revfore Accelerate before they can be configured in a page.

docs.title: Related Application Groups
docs.text:
• Use the Admin application group to create and manage Relational Tables, Models, and Views.
  • Use the Config application group for setup, upgrades, and system-level configuration.
  • Use the Help application group for documentation and support resources.

docs.title: Models
docs.text:
Models are the foundation of all relational data structures in Revfore Accelerate.

They define how your business data is stored, organized, and managed.

docs.title: 🧠 What is a Relationship?
docs.text:
A Relationship represents a business entity and contains a set of records (rows) and attributes (columns).

Examples of common Models:

  • Employees
  • Assets
  • Products
  • Services
  • Locations

Each Relationship is designed to capture data at its natural level of detail.

docs.title: 🧱 Relationship Structure
docs.text: A Relationship consists of:

docs.title: Columns (Fields)
docs.text:
Each column defines a specific attribute of the data.

Example (Employee Relationship):

  • Employee Name
  • Department
  • Role
  • Salary

docs.title: Rows (Records)
docs.text:
Each row represents an individual instance of that entity.

Example:

Employee Name Department Role Salary John Smith Finance Analyst 75,000 Jane Doe HR Manager 95,000

docs.title: ⚙️ How Models Work in Revfore Accelerate
docs.text:
Models in Revfore Accelerate are:

  • Defined through metadata (no SQL required)
  • EdiRelationship through a common UI
  • Reusable across workflows and dashboards
  • Integrated with Models and views

Once a Relationship is defined, it becomes part of a broader relational model.

docs.title: 🔗 Models and Models
docs.text:
Models rarely exist in isolation.

They are typically connected to other Models using Models.

Example:

  • Employee → Department
  • Asset → Location
  • Product → Service Line

These Models allow you to build more powerful and flexible data models.

docs.title: Think in Business Terms
docs.text:
Define Models based on real-world entities, not technical structures.

✔️ Good: - Employee - Asset - Product

❌ Avoid: - Generic or unclear Models like "Data1" or "Misc"

docs.title: Keep Models Focused
docs.text:
Each Relationship should represent one primary concept.

Avoid combining unrelated data into a single Relationship.

docs.title: Use Clear Naming
docs.text: Use names that are: - Easy to understand - Consistent across your model

docs.title: Plan for Reuse
docs.text: Models should be designed so they can be reused across: - Planning models - Dashboards - Workflows

docs.title: 🚀 Example Use Case
docs.text:
Let’s say you want to plan labor costs.

You might create:

Employee Relationship - Employee Name - Department - Role - Base Salary

Then: - Link to a Department Relationship - Use the data in planning workflows - Build views for reporting

docs.title: 💡 Tips
docs.text:
• Start with your core entities first
  • Add additional attributes over time
  • Avoid overcomplicating your initial design
  • Keep your model flexible

docs.title: 👉 Next Steps
docs.text:
• Learn about Relational Tables
  • Explore Relational Views

docs.title: Tables
docs.text:
Tables are the foundation of all relational data structures in Revfore Accelerate.

They define how your business data is stored, organized, and managed.

docs.title: 🧠 What is a Table?
docs.text:
A table represents a business entity and contains a set of records (rows) and attributes (columns).

Examples of common tables:

  • Employees
  • Assets
  • Products
  • Services
  • Locations

Each table is designed to capture data at its natural level of detail.

docs.title: 🧱 Table Structure
docs.text: A table consists of:

docs.title: Columns (Fields)
docs.text:
Each column defines a specific attribute of the data.

Example (Employee table):

  • Employee Name
  • Department
  • Role
  • Salary

docs.title: Rows (Records)
docs.text:
Each row represents an individual instance of that entity.

Example:

Employee Name Department Role Salary John Smith Finance Analyst 75,000 Jane Doe HR Manager 95,000

docs.title: ⚙️ How Tables Work in Revfore Accelerate
docs.text:
Tables in Revfore Accelerate are:

  • Defined through metadata (no SQL required)
  • Editable through a common UI
  • Reusable across workflows and dashboards
  • Integrated with relationships and views

Once a table is defined, it becomes part of a broader relational model.

docs.title: 🔗 Tables and Relationships
docs.text:
Tables rarely exist in isolation.

They are typically connected to other tables using relationships.

Example:

  • Employee → Department
  • Asset → Location
  • Product → Service Line

These relationships allow you to build more powerful and flexible data models.

docs.title: Think in Business Terms
docs.text:
Define tables based on real-world entities, not technical structures.

✔️ Good: - Employee - Asset - Product

❌ Avoid: - Generic or unclear tables like "Data1" or "Misc"

docs.title: Keep Tables Focused
docs.text:
Each table should represent one primary concept.

Avoid combining unrelated data into a single table.

docs.title: Use Clear Naming
docs.text: Use names that are: - Easy to understand - Consistent across your model

docs.title: Plan for Reuse
docs.text: Tables should be designed so they can be reused across: - Planning models - Dashboards - Workflows

docs.title: 🚀 Example Use Case
docs.text:
Let’s say you want to plan labor costs.

You might create:

Employee Table - Employee Name - Department - Role - Base Salary

Then: - Link to a Department table - Use the data in planning workflows - Build views for reporting

docs.title: 💡 Tips
docs.text:
• Start with your core entities first
  • Add additional attributes over time
  • Avoid overcomplicating your initial design
  • Keep your model flexible

docs.title: 👉 Next Steps
docs.text:
• Learn about Relational Models
  • Explore Relational Views

docs.title: Views
docs.text:
Views are the foundation of all relational data structures in Revfore Accelerate.

They define how your business data is stored, organized, and managed.

docs.title: 🧠 What is a View?
docs.text:
A View represents a business entity and contains a set of records (rows) and attributes (columns).

Examples of common Views:

  • Customers
  • Carriers

Each View is designed to capture data at its natural level of detail.

docs.title: 🧱 View Structure
docs.text: A View consists of:

docs.title: Columns (Fields)
docs.text:
Each column defines a specific attribute of the data.

Example (Employee View):

  • Employee Name
  • Department
  • Role
  • Salary

docs.title: Rows (Records)
docs.text:
Each row represents an individual instance of that entity.

Example:

Employee Name Department Role Salary John Smith Finance Analyst 75,000 Jane Doe HR Manager 95,000

docs.title: ⚙️ How Views Work in Revfore Accelerate
docs.text:
Views in Revfore Accelerate are:

  • Defined through metadata (no SQL required)
  • EdiView through a common UI
  • Reusable across workflows and dashboards
  • Integrated with relationships and views

Once a View is defined, it becomes part of a broader relational model.

docs.title: 🔗 Views and Relationships
docs.text:
Views rarely exist in isolation.

They are typically connected to other Views using relationships.

Example:

  • Employee → Department
  • Asset → Location
  • Product → Service Line

These relationships allow you to build more powerful and flexible data models.

docs.title: Think in Business Terms
docs.text:
Define Views based on real-world entities, not technical structures.

✔️ Good: - Employee - Asset - Product

❌ Avoid: - Generic or unclear Views like "Data1" or "Misc"

docs.title: Keep Views Focused
docs.text:
Each View should represent one primary concept.

Avoid combining unrelated data into a single View.

docs.title: Use Clear Naming
docs.text: Use names that are: - Easy to understand - Consistent across your model

docs.title: Plan for Reuse
docs.text: Views should be designed so they can be reused across: - Planning models - Dashboards - Workflows

docs.title: 🚀 Example Use Case
docs.text:
Let’s say you want to plan labor costs.

You might create:

Employee View - Employee Name - Department - Role - Base Salary

Then: - Link to a Department View - Use the data in planning workflows - Build views for reporting

docs.title: 💡 Tips
docs.text:
• Start with your core entities first
  • Add additional attributes over time
  • Avoid overcomplicating your initial design
  • Keep your model flexible

docs.title: 👉 Next Steps
docs.text:

  • Explore Relational Views

docs.title: Setup and Installation
docs.text: This guide walks you through installing and configuring Revfore Accelerate in your OneStream environment.

docs.title: 📦 Dependencies
docs.text:
Revfore Accelerate requires:

  • OneStream version 9.1.1 or later
  • A standard OneStream installation (no additional external dependencies required)

docs.title: Security Requirements
docs.text: The user installing the solution will need to be part of the Administrator security group.

docs.title: Step 1: Import Solution Package
docs.text:
• Download the Revfore Accelerate solution package from the OneStream Solution Exchange
  • Import it into your OneStream application by going to Application -> Tools -> Load/Extract and loading the zip file.

docs.title: Step 2: Request and Install Starter License Key
docs.text:
• Request a Starter License Key from OneStream which will allow you to use the Designer pages
  • Provide your Customer Name, located in:
  • Application → Application Properties
  • Once received:
  • Paste the key into the License Key field
  • Click Validate

docs.title: Step 3: Setup Database
docs.text:
• This step initializes the solution by:
  • Creating all required relational tables
  • Creating views and supporting objects
  • Seeding base configuration data

docs.title: Step 4: Launch Solution
docs.text:
• If not already there, Open the Revfore Accelerate Designer dashboard
  • Select Admin from the main menu:
  • Begin building your own relational solution by creating new tables, models, and views
  • Note: You will need a Full license key before you can use the Views in the end-user Navigation pages

docs.title: Step 5: Request and Install Full License Key
docs.text:
• A Full License Key is required for full functionality including the Navigation pages
  • To request:
  • Navigate to: Config → Manage License Key
  • Copy the Customer Reference Code

  • Send it to OneStream to request a Full License Key

  • Once received:

  • Paste the key into the License Key field
  • Click Validate
  • You can now use any of the Relational Views in the end-user Navigation pages

docs.title: 💡 Tips
docs.text:
• Review existing Relational Table, Model and View definitions to get familiar with them
  • Start with a simple use case (e.g., rate tables or reference data)

docs.title: 👉 Next Steps
docs.text:
• Return to the Getting Started Guide
  • Learn about Relational Tables
  • Explore Relational Models
  • Build your first Relation Views
